:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

New Court Commitments vs. Parole Revocations

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns/revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole. Understanding the relative proportion of these two groups is pivotal for evaluating the effectiveness of a state's criminal justice policies.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

By law, a nonviolent offender convicted of a Class D felony with an aggregate sentence of one to five years is eligible for parole review after serving 15% or two months of their original sentence, whichever is longer. The time before the first parole review for all felonies committed after December 3, 1980, is determined based on the sentence being served. For sentences ranging from 1 year, up to but not including 2 years, the time before the first review is 4 months. For sentences ranging from 2 years up to and including 39 years, the time before the first review is 20% of the sentence. For sentences of 39+ years, including life sentences, the time before the first review is 8 years. In the case of a persistent felony offender with a class A, B, or C felony, the time before the first review is 10 years.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 5,922 people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new crimes, with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up 32% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 75% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 90% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 36% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated with an original sentence length of 5-9.9 years, accounting for 40% of this group.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated for offenses related to property, accounting for 21% of this group.


``` ::: :::

Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date. Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date. Similarly, 80% of women and 74% of men were released before/on parole eligibility date

``` ::: :::

:::: ::: citation-section **Notes:** NCRP data is from 2020. The parole-eligible prison population only includes people with sentence lengths ranging between 1-25 years and people incarcerated for a new crime.
